name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}, {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "tool_calls": [{"id": "call_aZ3nnOU4PoaIJEia9ODwvaMp", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_aSgESzvXKXNvJEvpu007o8eL", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_LUq0Ee3O3DzK37dI6YABXuwE", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_top", "arguments": "{"all_namespaces": false, "namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_IB3hUvGWhUycN7kmXLlFpiy8", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "list_metrics", "arguments": "{"limit": 50, "offset": 0, "filter_pattern": "chaos|fault|inject|disrupt", "refresh_cache": false}"}}]}, {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_aZ3nnOU4PoaIJEia9ODwvaMp", "content": "NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS        AGE   IP             NODE                                NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS

input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "description": "List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "namespaces_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_log", "description": "Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get logs from"}, "query": {"type": "string", "description": "query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")"}, "tailLines": {"type": "integer", "description": "Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)", "default": 100, "minimum": 0}}, "required": ["name", "query"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_stats_summary", "description": "Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get stats from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_top", "description": "List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"label_selector": {"type": "string", "description": "Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)", "pattern": "^([/_.\-A-Za-z0-9=, ()!])+$"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_delete", "description": "Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod to delete"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to delete the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_exec", "description": "Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"command": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}, "description": "Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]"}, "container": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod where the command will be executed"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed"}}, "required": ["name", "command"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_get", "description": "Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to get the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"fieldSelector": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/", "pattern": "^[.\-A-Za-z0-9]+([=!,]{1,2}[.\-A-Za-z0-9]+)+$"}, "labelSelector": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label", "pattern": "^([/_.\-A-Za-z0-9=, ()!])+$"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "description": "List all the Kubernetes pods in the specified namespace in the current cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"fieldSelector": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools.       "Presence of a flash-agent pod suggests possible chaos/experimentation activity, but events do not explicitly show active chaos faults; issues should be treated as real but low-impact faults."
    ],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "partial",

      ],

    }

      "Namespace sock-shop currently has 16 pods, all in Running status with Ready containers.",
      "flash-agent-768c8f4599-29shp is a non-sock-shop app with 2/2 containers and 1 restart ~5 minutes ago, likely an agent or experiment/chaos-related component.",

      "Events show a Warning Unhealthy event for carts due to a failed startup probe (connection refused on /health), but this appears transient since the pod is now Ready.",
      "All other core microservices (carts, carts-db, catalogue, catalogue-db, front-end, orders, orders-db, payment, queue-master, rabbitmq, shipping, user-db) are Running with 0 restarts.",
      "No events mentioning Inject, Kill, Chaos, Disrupt, or Fault were visible in the portion of the events log provided; only normal scheduling, pulling, and startup events plus one Unhealthy warning.",
      "Cluster metrics via pods_top are forbidden for this service account in namespace sock-shop, and the Prometheus list_metrics tool is not available, limiting quantitative resource analysis."
    ],
    "analysis_approach": [
      "Listed all pods in namespace sock-shop and inspected their phase, Ready status, restarts, and labels to assess fleet health and detect unusual owners/agents.",
      "Listed events scoped to namespace sock-shop to identify warnings, errors, and any chaos-related markers (Inject, Kill, Chaos, Disrupt, Fault) and to correlate with pods.",
      "Attempted to fetch per-pod resource metrics via pods_top with namespace=sock-shop; this failed due to RBAC (metrics.k8s.io forbidden).",
      "Attempted to discover chaos/fault-related metrics via list_metrics; this tool is not available, so metric-backed chaos detection is not possible.",
      "Inferred health from pod readiness, restart counts, and events, and classified issues by severity and likely category (real fault vs external disturbance/chaos)."
    ],
    "observability_gaps": [
      "pods_top in namespace sock-shop is forbidden for the current service account (no access to metrics.k8s.io).",
      "Prometheus list_metrics tool is not available, preventing metric catalogue inspection and chaos metric detection.",

    },

      "recommended_action": "Review the carts pod’s startup and readiness probe configuration to ensure the initialDelaySeconds and timeoutSeconds are appropriate for its startup time.       "category": "external-disturbance",
      "summary": "flash-agent pod (2/2 containers, 1 recent restart) is running in the sock-shop namespace with labels app=flash-agent, indicating an external agent or experiment framework operating alongside the application.",
      "recommended_action": "Treat flash-agent as an external/agent component rather than part of the core sock-shop workload. Monitor its behavior but avoid deleting or scaling it without confirming its purpose with the platform/chaos/observability team. If it is a chaos or test agent, coordinate test windows to avoid overlapping with critical production traffic."
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "The sock-shop namespace is generally healthy: all core microservice pods are Running, and only minor instability is observed in the user service and a transient startup issue in carts. An external flash-agent pod is present, likely related to experimentation or observability. Lack of metrics-server access and incomplete event data slightly reduce confidence but do not indicate major problems.",
    "concerns": [

      "Transient startup probe failure on carts suggests that probe timing may be tight relative to application startup time.",
      "Presence of an external flash-agent in the same namespace may introduce additional load or disturbances if it is performing tests, though no explicit chaos events are visible.",
      "Absence of live resource metrics (CPU/memory) and chaos metrics limits the ability to detect resource saturation or ongoing experiments."
    ],
    "recommendations": [
      "Investigate the user service pod: pull recent logs and describe the pod to determine why it restarted and why it is currently not Ready; fix configuration or code issues and redeploy if necessary.",
      "Review and, if needed, tune startup/readiness probes for carts (and other services) to better match real startup times and avoid false Unhealthy events.",
      "Confirm the role of flash-agent with the owning team (e.g., SRE, chaos, or observability team). If it is a chaos/flash test agent, ensure its experiments are scheduled and documented so that observed issues can be correctly attributed.",
      "Implement or verify higher-level monitoring (e.g., HTTP checks against front-end and key APIs) to catch any user-facing impact from the user service instability.",
      "Consider adding alerts on pod restart rates and Ready status for critical services (user, front-end, orders, payment) using existing Prometheus/alerting infrastructure, since direct metrics-server access is not available to this agent."
    ],

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "description": "List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "namespaces_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_log", "description": "Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get logs from"}, "query": {"type": "string", "description": "query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")"}, "tailLines": {"type": "integer", "description": "Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)", "default": 100, "minimum": 0}}, "required": ["name", "query"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_stats_summary", "description": "Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get stats from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_top", "description": "List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"label_selector": {"type": "string", "description": "Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)", "pattern": "^([/_.\-A-Za-z0-9=, ()!])+$"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_delete", "description": "Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod to delete"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to delete the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_exec", "description": "Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"command": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}, "description": "Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]"}, "container": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod where the command will be executed"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed"}}, "required": ["name", "command"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_get", "description": "Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to get the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"fieldSelector": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/", "pattern": "^[.\-A-Za-z0-9]+([=!,]{1,2}[.\-A-Za-z0-9]+)+$"}, "labelSelector": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label", "pattern": "^([/_.\-A-Za-z0-9=, ()!])+$"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "description": "List all the Kubernetes pods in the specified namespace in the current cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"fieldSelector": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}, {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "tool_calls": [{"id": "call_91zjhAMJaMqoquaE1zJd8hlt", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_13C6w62Epu7EC4RjgEDm3RBu", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_XrmZtYgsyjreW5L0nApt86Ji", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_top", "arguments": "{"all_namespaces": false, "namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_kWPB2CXV0sz3J177nUeCDqOx", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "list_metrics", "arguments": "{"limit": 1000, "filter_pattern": "chaos|fault|inject|disrupt", "refresh_cache": false}"}}]}, {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_91zjhAMJaMqoquaE1zJd8hlt", "content": "NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS        AGE   IP             NODE                                NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS

sock-shop   v1           Pod    carts-db-794bdddcd7-cqwlq                1/1     Running   0               17m   10.244.0.100   aks-agentpool-17671638-vmss000014   <none>           <none>            app=sock-shop,name=carts-db,pod-template-hash=794bdddcd7
sock-shop   v1           Pod    catalogue-6c4df7bd4d-wmztx               1/1     Running   0               17m   10.244.0.216   aks-agentpool-17671638-vmss000014   <none>           <none>            app=sock-shop,name=catalogue,pod-template-hash=6c4df7bd4d

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools.
